next_action: Fourier decision RESOLVED by user 2026-07-07: dropped from build path (option a). FID generation-quality check now done, resolving the mask-only vs gaussian+mask practical-recipe question (see gates.generation_quality_fid). Result: mask-only wins masked-recovery but costs ~67 FID points vs gaussian+mask's ~4 points, at a masked-recovery cost of only 0.104 normalized_gap (0.568 vs 0.464). Next: this is a real tradeoff, not a clean winner -- decide whether the paper story is (a) mask-only as a specialized inpainting/recovery-focused variant (large recovery win, generation-quality caveat), (b) gaussian+mask as the general-purpose recipe (small but real generation-quality-preserving recovery gain), or (c) present both as separate contributions. All single-seed sanity-scale still -- Phase 2 multi-seed confirmation (per summer-2026-plan.md) needed on whichever arm(s) are chosen before this becomes a paper claim. Human input requested via pi-updates.md draft, not yet sent.

gates.step0_smoke: PASS - all 4 corruption modes run without crash, finite loss, on real IN-1K
gates.step2_mask_sanity: PASS (2026-07-06) - masked-recovery eval, mask_prob=0.5, n=256 held-out IN-1K val images, seed=0 (RNG-matched across arms). mean_masked_mse: gaussian floor=0.24237, mask treatment=0.11313, VAE-roundtrip oracle=0.01488. normalized_gap = (floor-treatment)/(floor-oracle) = 0.568. Mask-trained model recovers masked regions ~2.1x better than gaussian-trained model (57% of the floor-to-oracle headroom closed) -- masking the training start-state teaches the model to use visible context, which pure-Gaussian training never sees. Loss-curve comparison from earlier (mask ~5.2 vs gaussian ~11.2) is NOT part of this verdict -- retracted separately as an easiness artifact of the training target, unrelated to this outcome-metric-based PASS.
gates.step3_fourier_sanity: RESOLVED, NEGATIVE (2026-07-06) - isolated fourier-corruption training (fourier-cutoff=0.25), same eval protocol as step2. mean_masked_mse=0.40612, WORSE than gaussian floor (0.24237). normalized_gap=-0.720 (negative -- fourier-trained model is worse at masked-recovery than a model that never saw structured corruption at all). Mechanism read: fourier low-pass corruption teaches recovery of missing HIGH-FREQUENCY detail from coarse structure, not recovery of a spatially-masked region from surrounding visible pixels -- these are different tasks, and training on one does not transfer to (and may actively hurt, via distribution mismatch) the other. USER DECISION 2026-07-07: fourier dropped from build path entirely (option a of pi-updates.md ask).
gates.step4_mixture_arm_B: RESOLVED (2026-07-06) - 2-way gaussian+mask mixture (weights 1.0/1.0/0.0), same eval protocol. mean_masked_mse=0.13692, normalized_gap=0.464. Falls between isolated mask (0.568) and 3-arm mixture (0.449) as expected, but notably closer to the 3-arm mixture than to isolated mask -- confirms the 3-arm mixture's underperformance vs isolated mask is NOT primarily fourier-specific drag (arm B still loses ~0.10 gap vs pure mask despite excluding fourier entirely). Read together with step3's negative fourier result: the gap-closing recipe (gaussian:mask:fourier 1:1:1 vs 1:1:0 vs mask-only) suggests BOTH effects are real -- gradient-budget dilution (any mixing with gaussian reduces the pure-mask signal) AND fourier's own negative contribution. Fourier's negative pull is smaller in magnitude here (only 1/3 weight) than shown by its isolated -0.720 result, consistent with a real but diluted drag.
gates.generation_quality_fid: RESOLVED (2026-07-07) - sanity FID (2000 samples, unconditional, GD sampler 250 steps, single seed) on gaussian/mask/arm-B checkpoints. Smoke-sample-probe passed (eyeballed generated images: coherent textured scenes, not garbage/noise -- confirms sampler correctness at this early-training FID range). Results: gaussian floor FID=172.567, mask-only FID=239.512 (+66.9), gaussian+mask arm B FID=176.711 (+4.1). CONFIRMS user's hypothesis: mask-only trades substantial generation quality for its larger masked-recovery gain (0.568 vs arm B's 0.464, a 0.104 difference) -- gaussian+mask nearly preserves baseline generation quality while capturing 82% of mask-only's recovery gain. This is the key generalization-vs-specialization tradeoff the user asked to test; it is a genuine tradeoff, not a clean winner for either recipe.

needs_user_input.prompt: Fourier question resolved (dropped). New decision point from the FID result: mask-only gets the best masked-recovery score (normalized_gap=0.568) but costs ~67 FID points vs baseline; gaussian+mask (arm B) nearly preserves generation quality (+4 FID) at a smaller recovery gain (0.464). See documentation/pi-updates.md for full write-up. Ask: should the paper story be (a) mask-only as a specialized recovery/inpainting variant with an explicit generation-quality caveat, (b) gaussian+mask as the general-purpose recipe (safer, smaller effect size), (c) present both as separate contributions/recipes for different use cases, or (d) explore mask-heavy mixtures (3:1, 4:1 mask:gaussian, as previously proposed) to find a better point on this tradeoff curve before deciding? No further compute committed pending this decision.
